target.name: Cerebras
target.founder: Andrew Feldman, Gary Lauterbach, Michael James, Sean Lie, Jean-Philippe Fricker

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Chips & Semiconductors. Range: $1.0B - $5.0B.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Semiconductors P/S median is 25.3x across 3 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

private.name: Credo Semiconductor
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Public), post-money $5B+

private.name: Ouster
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Public), post-money $1.5B

private.name: Aeva Technologies
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Public), post-money $1B+

data.narrative:
Cerebras is a public-stage semiconductor company founded by Andrew Feldman and four co-founders, having raised over $1.1B to date. The valuation analysis draws from a universe of 3 same-sector public comparables in Chips & Semiconductors: Credo Semiconductor ($5.0B), Ouster ($1.5B), and Aeva Technologies ($1.0B).

The peer-median method is applicable and yields a $1.5B implied valuation based on the median across these three public-stage semiconductor peers, with a range spanning $1.0B to $5.0B. A public-comp anchoring approach using broader sector multiples—the Semiconductors universe trades at a median P/S of 25.3x across ALAB, NVDA, and AMD—cannot be applied due to absence of Cerebras revenue data on file, a common constraint in private company analysis.

Triangulation across the single applicable method supports a valuation range of $1.3B (low) to $1.7B (high), with a median of $1.5B. The current public valuation of $23B materially exceeds this range, reflecting either significant revenue scale or growth expectations not captured in the peer median. Key uncertainties include proprietary revenue figures, market penetration relative to peers, and whether the peer set adequately captures Cerebras's differentiation in the semiconductor subsector. The narrow method count (1) limits conviction; revenue disclosure would enable P/S triangulation.
